item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmmh, I didn't really feel it. I would have loved to see more on when this thing was unleashed instead of these descriptions kind of going over what could happen. Things like the rewriting of thaumaturgic laws and the seven keys sound really interesting but we don't get a whole lot of detail on that. I think this suffers from not following that writers adage of writing the most interesting part of your characters life. This suggests a lot of cool stuff but ultimately doesn't deliver the goods. Also, I really dislike Obskura and its inclusion did not bring any good will in me towards this SCP but it's not why I downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless "Obskuracorps" is a translated term, it should be "Obskurakorps" in German^^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What really sold me on this is how it has to be contained. Tentatively working with people who aren't exactly allies while not being able to share information with them is a really good move considering the world your team has built up, as well as the total information suppression of such an item.

Two questions barely related to this article:

  1. Shouldn't Obskura be a tag for this skip? I know they're not super involved here, but they're mentioned enough and have had enough of an influence on the history of this SCP that a tag feels warranted here.
  2. Unrelated sort of, but the link to sirpudding's Unexplained Location entry for this contest does not direct to it. I'm having trouble finding it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've re-read this now that I understand what's going on (thanks, commenters!) and decided to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Helluva storyline. It was a bold choice going after a historical averted doomsday, and it really payed off while giving your team a distinct flavor from the rest of us. Well done to all of you, this was top quality entertainment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat.

Given that this is basically describing a massive arcane ritual in clinical tone, this does a fairly solid job. Additionally, I totally buy into the GOC and Foundation cooperation here, given that the alternative is massive magical mayhem.

I do wish that a list of known artifacts that could be used for the ritual was included here, as that is one aspect of this particular team that I find rather intriguing. However, there is something to be said about being hooked enough on the series to want to delve deeper down the rabbit hole to find out about that info.

+1. Well done CS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GR-Class Hostile Demiurge Event

And there's our event class. :) Of course, the naming makes me think that once you use this to summon a god, it shows up like, "I'm a hostile demiurge! GRRRRR!" :V

If I was going to upvote for one thing, it's that sting operation where they're trying to bait people trying to enact the ritual. I've never seen that level of direct cooperation between the Foundation and GoIs before, and I love it, it's perfect. I also love how they're like, "Let's just gently urge everyone to stop using religion so this isn't effective."

I think footnote 2 should be moved up a paragraph, since that's where the O-Korps are first mentioned.

Goddamn, this is a really good story this team is making, though. This is quickly becoming a favorite entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adding on to this, the concept comes from gnostic theodicy. Some gnostics sects thought that God that allows Evil to exist cannot be the same infinitely merciful and just God that sent the Christ Logos, and in particular the God of the Old Testament is often inexplicably incompetent or just awful. So they believed that the actual Alpha and Omega God can't interact directly with Creation without destroying it so it created along with the universe entities that could run the universe from within. One or more of these entities (depending on sect) eventually either deluded themselves or lied about being actual-God and became the demiurge(s). Incidently this is the origin of quite a lot of the metaphysics and terminology in the settings, like "Archons" (what some sects called the entities left to rule the universe) and Yalboath (what some sects called the demiurge).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the simplest terms, a demiurge is a lower-case-god; a creator but not the creator. The Architect from the Matrix Trilogy is a good example: an entity tasked with building and regulating the matrix from within the system. Powerful, but bound within its realm, and subservient to the actual creator(s) of the matrix, the machine hive mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, yeah by the time I had gotten to the end I had completely forgotten the fact that the first part creates a new entity and doesn't give those powers to an existing person - I think the mention of the previous demiurge threw me off. That actually does clarify a few of my issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think demiurge = deity in this context, the ceremony essentially creates a god that can be bent (to some extent) to human will. I don't think the ceremony is capable of bestowing power on an existing being, it purely creates a new one in order to enact the changes to thaumaturgy. Without the second part to control how the demiurge acts, you are just unleashing a new god upon the world. Gods, especially in the SCP universe, have their own motivations and one that is capable of fundamentally altering how magic works would be an extremely serious threat upon the world. Finally, since there is no canon and magic is, well, magic, the rules behind thaumaturgy are a bit vague BUT the art does appear to always use this blue energy as its power source and I think, even without understanding every rule behind it, we can infer that this ritual could change how rituals occur, make established methods of "spellcasting" create new or undesired effects, etc. in addition to changing the distribution of thaumaturgic power across the world/universe. My best guess anyway. As a side note, +1 to this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having trouble summing up my thoughts on this one. I mostly liked it, but I have so many questions that I'm just sort of baffled.

- What's a demiurge? Unless I'm being uncredibly dense you don't actually define what that term means anywhere.

- If the demiurge is already on your side, do you need the 2nd part to control it? Or, if you were going to perform this thing, why wouldn't you just make yourself the demiurge? Is there a downside to it? I'm not really getting why a part of this is something to control the thing, when actually becoming the thing yourself seems better.

- In what ways can thaumaturgy be altered by this? I don't really get the implications of it because I don't know enough about thaumaturgy mechanically (beyond "its magic"). I get that being the demiurge basically means you can transfer this thaumaturgic power to other people, but I don't really get the significance or implication behind being able to otherwise alter it.

I feel like I'm missing a lot of the information needed to actually understand this properly.

ETA: Turns out im rather dense, see below.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
What's this, Chao wrote a scip? We truly do live in an age of wonder and miracles. I guess the lesson to be learned here is that to get me to write something there needs to be a team contest.
First and foremost let me thank my team members 9Volt, sirpudding and GreenWolf for their help with the article and the fun discussions about our team's entries. Extra thanks go to GreenWolf, who tirelessly helped me iron out my tone and phrasing. English is hard. Further feedback thanks go to TyGently, DrMagnus,A Random Day, minmin and Taffeta.

This is the final entry of our doomcon storyline, answering the question "What actually is that ominous Rite that everybody seems to be up in arms about?". Due to scheduling conflicts there's going to be another entry later today that fits between this and 9Volt's tales, so stay tuned for that!

Image source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Iron_Crown.JPG
